title: Battenberg cake
subtitle: The chequered pink and yellow sponge wrapped in marzipan
excerpt: A Battenberg made with two colour-striped Victoria sponges cut into four long logs, rearranged into the chequered pattern, stuck together with apricot jam, and wrapped tightly in a sheet of marzipan. The pattern shows on every slice. Serves 10.
sourceNotes: The Battenberg cake is documented in British baking from the late nineteenth century, with various accounts linking it to the 1884 marriage of Queen Victoria's granddaughter to Prince Louis of Battenberg. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and its later editions document the marzipan-wrapped loaf cake concept. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the almond paste (marzipan) preparation is documented in detail. The two-sponge construction with a jam filling and external marzipan covering is a Victorian innovation documented in British domestic cookery literature from the 1890s onward.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponges bake up to 2 days ahead; wrap and store at room temperature. Assemble with marzipan on the day of serving.

recipeTools.notes: 20 × 20 cm square tin, or a standard Battenberg tin with the divider. A standard rectangular tin works if you construct a foil divider down the centre.
For measuring precise 5 cm × 5 cm logs.

glossaryTerms.definition: Rolling marzipan into a thin rectangular sheet and using it to encase a loaf cake or assembled sponge. The marzipan sheet must be large enough to wrap fully around the cake with a seam join on the base. Dust the work surface with icing sugar to prevent sticking; press the seam firmly to seal.

content.text: A Battenberg is a cake built in three stages: bake two long strips of sponge in different colours, cut them into precise logs, rearrange them into the chequered pattern with jam, then encase the whole thing in marzipan. The pattern , which shows two pink squares diagonally opposite two yellow squares on every cut face, requires some precision with the knife and the ruler, but no particular skill beyond patience.

The sponge is a standard Victoria ratio baked in a 20 × 20 cm tin divided down the centre. One half is left plain; the other is coloured pink with food colouring. After baking and cooling, each half is cut lengthways into two equal strips (four strips total) which are rearranged and stuck together with apricot jam. The wrapped marzipan needs to be firm enough to hold the shape when sliced. Refrigerate the assembled cake for 30 minutes before cutting.

attrs.title: The foil divider
attrs.body: If you don't have a Battenberg tin, make a foil divider: fold a double sheet of foil into a strip about 5 cm wide and 20 cm long, fold it in half to make a wall, and press it into the centre of the greased tin. The sponge batter goes either side. It won't be as precise as a tin divider, but works well.

items.prepNote: pink or red gel food colouring

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease a 20 × 20 cm square tin and line with baking paper. Fit the divider down the centre if using a standard tin. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} softened butter and {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar until pale and fluffy (about 4 minutes with a hand mixer). Add the {{eggs}} eggs one at a time, beating well after each. Fold in the {{self-raising-flour}} sifted self-raising flour and {{almond-extract}} almond extract. Divide the batter in half. Leave one half plain; colour the other half with enough {{food-colouring}} pink food colouring to make a deep, unambiguous pink (it will fade slightly in the oven — start darker than you think you need). Spoon each half into one side of the divided tin. Bake for 25–30 minutes, until the sponges are golden, spring back when pressed, and a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then transfer to a rack to cool completely.

Trim the sides and top of each sponge to make clean, flat edges. Each half should measure roughly 10 × 20 cm. Cut each half lengthways into two equal strips, about 5 cm wide each — four strips total: two plain and two pink. Trim the strips to the same height (about 5 cm) so they stack evenly.

Warm the {{jam}} apricot jam and pass it through a sieve to remove any lumps. Lay one plain and one pink strip side by side on the work surface. Brush the touching sides with apricot jam and press them together firmly. Brush the top of the two joined strips with jam. Place the remaining two strips on top in the opposite order (pink over plain, plain over pink) to create the chequered pattern. Press firmly so all four strips are bonded together in a tight 10 × 10 cm log. Wrap loosely in cling film and refrigerate for 20 minutes to firm up.

Dust the work surface with {{icing-sugar}} icing sugar. Roll the {{marzipan}} into a rectangle large enough to wrap fully around the log (roughly 35 × 20 cm, about 4 mm thick). Brush the outside of the sponge log all over with apricot jam. Place the log at one short end of the marzipan sheet, leaving a 2 cm margin. Roll the log in the marzipan, pressing firmly, until it is fully enclosed with the seam on the base. Press the seam firmly to seal and trim any excess marzipan from the ends. The
should be taut — a loose wrap crinkles when sliced. Refrigerate for 30 minutes before cutting.
Use a long sharp knife and cut decisively in one downward stroke — sawing compresses the pattern and smears the marzipan. Each slice should show four squares in alternating colours.

attrs.heading: When things go wrong
attrs.intro: Battenberg has two specific failure modes.

items.symptom: The pattern blurs when sliced — pink and yellow smear into each other
items.cause: Cake wasn't cold enough when sliced, or knife was dragged rather than pressed down.
items.fix: Refrigerate the assembled cake for at least 30 minutes before cutting. Use the longest sharp knife you have and cut in a single downward stroke, not a sawing motion.

items.symptom: Marzipan tears when rolling or crinkles after wrapping
items.cause: Marzipan dried out from the icing sugar, or the sheet was too thin.
items.fix: Knead the marzipan briefly before rolling — it should feel pliable and smooth. Don't over-dust with icing sugar; just enough to prevent sticking. The sheet should be 4 mm thick, not thinner.
